text: The surgeon demonstrates the inframesocolic approach, revealing the splenic flexure and the left mesocolon.

The surgeon begins the dissection by opening the left mesocolon near the inferior mesenteric vein. The Toes fascia is identified and maintained in a downward position as the dissection progresses, with the left mesocolon elevated.
The surgeon mobilizes the colonic splenic flexure, ensuring adequate length for the planned sigmoid or rectal resection to prevent anastomotic tension.

The surgeon continues dissection towards the transverse mesocolon, leaving the left kidney and Gerota's fascia posteriorly. The dissection reaches the left paracolic gutter, where the pancreatic body is identified to facilitate opening the transverse mesocolon and accessing the lesser sac. The transverse colon is sectioned using a coagulating hook, positioned just above the pancreatic body.
The surgeon continues dissection along the pancreatic body, reaching and completely freeing the pancreatic tail within the lesser sac.

The spleen is visible as the surgeon continues dissection towards the left paracolic gutter, freeing the left mesocolon. The left extremity of the transverse mesocolon is also released. The flexure mobilization proceeds with the lateral opening of the left paracolic gutter, aided by the pneumoperitoneum reaching the lateral abdominal wall.

The surgeon sections the parietocolic transparent fibers, revealing the spleen. The mobilization progresses with the sectioning of the gastrocolic ligament using a coagulating hook. After opening the first layer, the second layer is exposed and sectioned, completing the mobilization of the splenic flexure.
The gyrotous fascia, pancreatic body and tail, spleen, and stomach are visible. The colon is moved downward with ease, and a final view through the mesocolic window revisits the various organs.

The surgeon employs a coagulating hook to mobilize the gastrocolic ligament, staying close to the transverse colon. The first layer is cut, revealing the second layer, and the third layer is subsequently recognized.
The surgeon cuts through the fourth layer, gaining access to the lesser sac. The left paracolic gutter is sectioned, connecting to the previous opening, and Girota's fascia becomes visible.
The surgeon sections the attachments to the transverse mesocolon, reaching the beginning of the left mesocolon. The dissection continues medially, further freeing the left mesocolon.

The surgeon incises the paracordic gutter, freeing the colon while keeping the parietal fatty tissue laterally. The mobilization proceeds downward, re-exposing Girota's fascia and the left mesocolon. The left mesocolon, Girota's fascia, and the parietal wall are clearly identified as the mobilization continues, freeing the left mesocolon from its lateral attachments.

The surgeon reaches and exposes the pancreatic body while continuing the mobilization of the left paracolic gutter. The dissection is performed close to the left colon to avoid opening the parietal peritoneum, with a demonstration of incorrect dissection showing the opening of the parietal peritoneal sheet. The splenic flexure appears mobilized, allowing the entire left colon to be moved downwards. Girota's fascia is clearly visible, and if needed, the transverse mesocolon can be freed from the pancreatic body for additional distal length.

The gastric wall, lesser sac, and pancreatic body are clearly visible after completion of the dissection. The colon is shown in a downward position, and the third approach, lateral to medial, is initiated.
The surgeon begins the procedure by incising the left paracolic gutter using a coagulating hook, noting the presence of a high splenic flexure.

The surgeon displays the left mesocolon and frees it from the parietal wall, revealing Girota's fascia. Incision of the tissue just superior to Girota's fascia exposes the spleen, while the fatty tissue is separated from the parietal-peritoneal sheath.

The surgeon mobilizes the gastrocolic ligament, staying close to the colon. The dissection involves sectioning one layer and then the other, revealing the open lesser sac and identifying the posterior wall of the stomach. The gastrocolic ligament is further freed from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon sections the attachments to the pancreatic body, displaying the mobilized splenic flexure with the colon downloaded. The view includes Girota's fascia, the pancreatic body, and the posterior wall of the stomach. The transverse colon appears well freed, and the splenic flexure is repositioned to its original region.
